sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage: A catalyst was tested for decomposition of compound X. Without catalyst, the activation energy was estimated at 75 kJ/mol. With catalyst, the activation energy decreased to 45 kJ/mol, while the reaction enthalpy remained −20 kJ/mol. Product formation was faster in the catalyzed trial at all temperatures tested.

explanations.B: Correct. The passage reports that activation energy decreased from 75 to 45 kJ/mol when catalyst was added, while ΔH stayed −20 kJ/mol. Catalysts speed reactions by lowering the activation barrier.
explanations.A: The reaction enthalpy remained the same in both pathways.
explanations.C: No new product identity was described.
explanations.D: A catalyst changes pathway, not the overall balanced reaction.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 1. Activation energies and reaction enthalpy for catalyzed and uncatalyzed pathways.

questions.stem: Why does lowering activation energy increase rate?

choices.A: A larger fraction of molecules can reach the transition state

explanations.A: Correct. At a given temperature, fewer molecules can overcome a high energy barrier. Lowering Ea increases the fraction with enough energy to reach the transition state, increasing rate.
explanations.B: Catalysts do not make equilibrium constants zero.
explanations.C: The passage does not describe irreversible product consumption.
explanations.D: Collisions still occur; the catalyst changes the energetic pathway.

questions.stem: What happens to ΔH when the catalyst is used?

explanations.C: Correct. Reaction enthalpy depends on the energies of reactants and products, not the path between them. The passage states ΔH remained −20 kJ/mol with or without catalyst.
explanations.A: +45 kJ/mol is the catalyzed activation energy, not ΔH.
explanations.B: The temperature data do not indicate ΔH became zero.
explanations.D: Ea and ΔH are different quantities.

questions.stem: Will the catalyst change the equilibrium constant?

choices.D: No, it speeds forward and reverse processes without changing equilibrium position
choices.C: Only if the reaction is exothermic

explanations.D: Correct. A catalyst affects the pathway and rate of reaching equilibrium but not the free-energy difference between reactants and products. Therefore it does not change Keq.
explanations.A: Keq depends on thermodynamics, not catalyst presence.
explanations.B: There is no general catalyst-driven decrease in Keq.
explanations.C: Exothermicity does not make catalysts alter equilibrium constants.

questions.stem: Which diagram would best represent the catalyzed pathway?

choices.C: A lower peak with the same reactant and product energies

explanations.C: Correct. A catalyst lowers the activation barrier but leaves initial and final state energies unchanged. On an energy diagram, that appears as a lower transition-state peak with the same reactant and product levels.
explanations.A: Changing only product energy would change ΔH, which the passage says remained the same.
explanations.B: Changing reactant energy would also change ΔH or the baseline.
explanations.D: The reaction still proceeds through a transition state, just a lower-energy pathway.

questions.passage: An esterification mixture contained acetic acid, ethanol, ethyl acetate, and water at equilibrium. Removing water shifted the mixture toward more ester product. Heating the exothermic reaction decreased the equilibrium yield of ester. Students tracked ester concentration under three conditions: baseline, water removed, and heated.
questions.stem: Removing water shifts the reaction toward:

explanations.B: Correct. Water is a product of esterification. Removing a product causes the system to shift toward products to replace it, increasing ester concentration.
explanations.A: A shift toward reactants would produce more water rather than replace removed water by forward reaction.
explanations.C: The plotted ester concentration changes, so there is a shift.
explanations.D: No catalyst is described as the equilibrium target.

questions.passageImageCaption: Figure 2. Ester concentration after equilibrium under different perturbations.

questions.stem: Why does heating reduce ester yield if the reaction is exothermic?

choices.A: Heat acts like a product, so adding heat favors reactants
choices.B: Heat acts like a reactant in exothermic reactions
choices.C: Temperature has no effect on Keq

explanations.A: Correct. For an exothermic forward reaction, heat can be treated as a product. Adding heat shifts equilibrium in the reverse direction, lowering ester yield.
explanations.B: In an exothermic forward reaction, heat is on the product side.
explanations.C: Temperature changes equilibrium constants for reactions with nonzero enthalpy change.
explanations.D: Heating does not specifically remove ethanol in the passage.

questions.stem: Which condition produced the highest ester concentration?

explanations.D: Correct. The figure shows ester concentration highest when water was removed, 0.61 M, compared with 0.42 M at baseline and 0.31 M when heated.
explanations.B: Heated gives the lowest ester concentration.

questions.stem: A catalyst added to this mixture would:

choices.B: Speed approach to equilibrium without changing the final ester concentration

explanations.B: Correct. A catalyst lowers activation energy for both directions and helps the mixture reach equilibrium faster. It does not change the equilibrium composition.
explanations.A: Keq is thermodynamic and not permanently increased by a catalyst.
explanations.C: A catalyst does not remove water unless it is also a drying agent, which is not stated.
explanations.D: The esterification remains a chemical reaction.

questions.stem: Which expression belongs in the numerator of Keq for esterification?

explanations.A: Correct. For acetic acid + ethanol ⇌ ethyl acetate + water, products appear in the numerator of Keq. Thus the numerator contains [ethyl acetate][water].
explanations.B: Acetic acid and ethanol are reactants, so they belong in the denominator.
explanations.C: This omits ethyl acetate and does not match the balanced equilibrium expression.
explanations.D: Catalysts are not included in the equilibrium expression.
